era_context: Late Bronze Age, Egypt under Ramesses II, Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II. Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIB. Shang Dynasty in China. This is the era of great imperial powers, international diplomacy, and the complex balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East before the Bronze Age collapse.

events.title: Ramesses II and Muwatalli II military preparations and territorial disputes
events.description: Both Ramesses II and Muwatalli II continue military preparations and maneuvers in Syria and the Levant, the contested zone between Egyptian and Hittite spheres of influence. Egyptian forces conduct campaigns in Nubia and the Levant, while Hittite forces maintain control over northern Syria and Anatolia. This period of military tension and diplomatic maneuvering precedes the major confrontation at Kadesh. Both powers maintain extensive military infrastructure and supply lines.

sources.name: Egyptian temple reliefs and administrative records
Hittite royal inscriptions and clay tablets

events.certainty_note: The military activities of both powers are well-documented, but precise year-by-year dating is uncertain (±2–3 years).

events.title: Mycenaean palace administration and Linear B record-keeping
events.description: Mycenaean palace centers maintain sophisticated administrative systems documented through Linear B tablets. These tablets record commodity distribution, labor organization, religious offerings, and military organization. The palaces function as economic and religious centers controlling extensive territories. This period represents the height of Mycenaean palace civilization before the Late Bronze Age collapse.

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Pylos, Mycenae, and Knossos

events.certainty_note: Mycenaean administrative practices are directly attested in Linear B tablets. The specific year assignment is approximate within Late Helladic IIIB (c. 1300–1190 BCE).

events.title: Shang Dynasty military campaigns and vassal control
events.region: China (Yellow River valley and surrounding regions)
events.description: The Shang Dynasty conducts military campaigns against neighboring peoples and maintains control over a network of vassal states. Oracle bones record military activities, including campaigns against the Shang's enemies. The Shang state maintains a standing army and conducts regular military expeditions to secure resources and maintain political control. This period represents the mature military organization of the Shang state.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of fortifications and weapons

events.certainty_note: Shang military organization is archaeologically confirmed. Precise year dating remains uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Ramesses II's military activities has been reassessed by recent scholarship. While traditionally portrayed as triumphant, some campaigns may have been less decisive than temple reliefs suggest, and some may have been defensive rather than offensive. The Hittite sources present a different perspective on the same conflicts, suggesting that neither power achieved decisive victory. The characterization of Mycenaean palace civilization as uniformly stable has been questioned by scholars who note signs of stress and instability in the archaeological record.
historiographic_note: This year's record is dominated by sources from literate imperial powers. Egyptian and Hittite sources present official narratives that may exaggerate military successes and downplay defeats. Mycenaean sources are limited to administrative tablets, which provide economic and organizational information but limited narrative context. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled much later. The vast majority of human societies in this year are invisible in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Military tensions between Egypt and the Hittites continue to escalate
Mycenaean palace civilization continues until disrupted by the Late Bronze Age collapse
